coredata.dc:title: Effect of doping of organo-soluble carbon dots on ionic relaxation and conductivity of planar anchored cyanobiphenyl based nematic liquid crystal
coredata.dc:creator: P, Priscilla
Singh, Ashwani Kumar
Malik, Praveen
Kumar, Sandeep
Supreet,
Gathania, Arvind K.
Prakash, Jai
Castagna, Riccardo
Lucchetta, Daniele Eugenio
Malik, Poonma
Singh, Gautam
coredata.dc:description: We demonstrate here the striking effect of organo-soluble carbon dots (CDs, diameter ∼7- 8 nm) on the tunability of ionic relaxation and conductivity of the host, planar anchored thermotropic nematic liquid crystal (5CB, 4-Cyano-4′-pentylbiphenyl). The optical texture, frequency, temperature, and concentration dependent dielectric studies of pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites have been performed using the polarising optical microscope and high-resolution dielectric spectroscopy, respectively. The doping of 0.5 wt% CDs into planar anchored 5CB is resulted into the vertical alignment confirmed through the cross-polarised optical microscope. The doping of CDs in 5CB liquid crystal is resulted into an unprecedented faster ionic relaxation with an abatement of the ionic amplitude at room temperature. The shift in the relaxation frequency for 0.5 wt% CDs-5CB composite (induced vertical alignment) as compared to pristine 5CB (planar aligned) at 28 °C (nematic) is found to be ∼13 folds which is further increased to ∼18 folds at 38 °C (isotropic). A similar trend in the conductivity curve is also observed which further confirms the significant enhancement in conductivity value with increase in temperature and concentration of dopant CDs. The observation of enhanced ionic conductivity and faster ionic relaxation in CDs-5CB composites as compared to pristine 5CB are attributed to the complete change of planar to vertical alignment by doping of CDs on planar anchored liquid crystal sample cells. It appears that the plausible strong interaction between hydrocarbon chains of CDs and 5CB molecules through anchored substrate surfaces with increasing concentration of CDs and decrease in rotational viscosity of host 5CB with increasing temperature certainly plays a pivotal role in the tuning of ionic relaxation and conductivity in the liquid crystal sample cells. We certainly believe that our results would catalyze the cognizance of the ionic relaxation and conductivity in CDs-5CB composites and other related systems. Moreover, such composites with tunable ionic relaxation/conductivity would be certainly helpful in the fabrication of various tuneable devices such as wave front corrector, low frequency oscillation generators, dynamic light scattering, and so on.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nematic liquid crystal
Carbon dots
Nanocomposites
Dielectric spectroscopy

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
Council of Science and Technology, U.P.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors Priscilla P and Gautam Singh thank to Amity University Uttar Pradesh, Noida, India for its continuous support and encouragement in their research work. Priscilla P would like to thank UGC for the fellowship awarded under the scheme Savitribai Jyotirao Phule Single Girl Child Fellowship ( SJSGC 2022-2023 ). Jai Prakash and Gautam Singh are grateful to the Council of Science and Technology (CST), U. P., India for financial assistance under CST funded research project (Project ID: 1960). Supreet would like to acknowledge the support provided under the DST-FIST Grant No.SR/FST/PS-I/2019/68 of Govt. of India.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The chemical structure of (a) carbon dots (CDs) [39] and (b) 5CB, LC.

Optical textures of (a) Pristine 5CB, (b) 0.03 wt%, (c) 0.05 wt%, (d) 0.1 wt%, (e) 0.25 wt% and (f) 0.5 wt% of CDs-5CB composites in the Nematic phase at 28 °C. P, A and R represents the polarizer, analyzer and rubbing direction of the cell, respectively. The thickness of planar anchored sample cells is 5.0 µm.

The frequency dependent (a) dielectric permittivity (ε′), inset shows dielectric loss (ε′′) with varying frequency, and (b) dielectric loss factor (tan δ) for pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites at T = 28 °C is shown. The cross-polarised optical texture for (i) 0.0 wt% (pristine), (ii) 0.25 wt% and (iii) 0.5 wt% CDs-5CB composites are shown as insets of the figure (b) (P and A denotes Polarizer and Analyzer, respectively).
The Cole-Cole plot of pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites at 28 °C. Inset portrays zoomed Cole-Cole plot.
The frequency and temperature dependent dielectric loss factor (tan δ) for (a) 0.0 wt% (pristine), (b) 0.03 wt%, (c) 0.05 wt%, (d) 0.1 wt%, (e) 0.25 wt% and (f) 0.5 wt% CDs-5CB composites are shown.

The temperature dependent ionic relaxation frequency (νR) for pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites. R2 in the graph denotes coefficient of determination. The symbols represent the experimental data and solid lines denote the best linear fit to the experimental data of each sample.

The angular frequency (ω) dependent conductivity (σ) for pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites at 28 °C is shown and fitted with Jonscher's Power law (JPL). The symbols and solid lines represent the experimental data and JPL fitting curves, respectively.

Schematic representation of the ionic motion in (a) planar aligned LC cell at E = 0 V (no oscillating voltage applied), (b) a planar aligned LC molecules where E ⊥ n, n denotes the director of LC molecules and (c) the induced vertical alignment produced by dopant CDs (E || n) which facilitates the ionic motion.

Fitted Arrhenius plot showing the variation of ln (σi) with respect to the inverse of absolute temperature having the value of R2 varying from 93 % to 99 % for pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites. The symbols represent the experimental data and solid lines denote the fitting with Arrhenius equation.
Concentration dependent activation energy (Ea) for pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites.

head.ce:title: Effect of doping of organo-soluble carbon dots on ionic relaxation and conductivity of planar anchored cyanobiphenyl based nematic liquid crystal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We demonstrate here the striking effect of organo-soluble carbon dots (CDs, diameter ∼7- 8 nm) on the tunability of ionic relaxation and conductivity of the host, planar anchored thermotropic nematic liquid crystal (5CB, 4-Cyano-4′-pentylbiphenyl). The optical texture, frequency, temperature, and concentration dependent dielectric studies of pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites have been performed using the polarising optical microscope and high-resolution dielectric spectroscopy, respectively. The doping of 0.5 wt% CDs into planar anchored 5CB is resulted into the vertical alignment confirmed through the cross-polarised optical microscope. The doping of CDs in 5CB liquid crystal is resulted into an unprecedented faster ionic relaxation with an abatement of the ionic amplitude at room temperature. The shift in the relaxation frequency for 0.5 wt% CDs-5CB composite (induced vertical alignment) as compared to pristine 5CB (planar aligned) at 28 °C (nematic) is found to be ∼13 folds which is further increased to ∼18 folds at 38 °C (isotropic). A similar trend in the conductivity curve is also observed which further confirms the significant enhancement in conductivity value with increase in temperature and concentration of dopant CDs. The observation of enhanced ionic conductivity and faster ionic relaxation in CDs-5CB composites as compared to pristine 5CB are attributed to the complete change of planar to vertical alignment by doping of CDs on planar anchored liquid crystal sample cells. It appears that the plausible strong interaction between hydrocarbon chains of CDs and 5CB molecules through anchored substrate surfaces with increasing concentration of CDs and decrease in rotational viscosity of host 5CB with increasing temperature certainly plays a pivotal role in the tuning of ionic relaxation and conductivity in the liquid crystal sample cells. We certainly believe that our results would catalyze the cognizance of the ionic relaxation and conductivity in CDs-5CB composites and other related systems. Moreover, such composites with tunable ionic relaxation/conductivity would be certainly helpful in the fabrication of various tuneable devices such as wave front corrector, low frequency oscillation generators, dynamic light scattering, and so on.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Centuries after the birth of liquid crystal (LC) science, even today it remains as a topic of trigger and engagement for both physicists and chemists around the globe. Though initially for some 75 years it remained as a subject of pure academic interest eventually the idea was bloomed to utilize it in display devices [1]. What makes LC unique is that they share the properties of both crystalline solids as well as isotropic fluids. Nematic liquid crystals (NLCs) have always been in use for the manufacturing of display and other photonic devices due to their simplest phase structure and easily tunable electro-optical and dielectric properties. NLC exhibits zero positional ordering however it shows a long-range orientational ordering, and in the bulk all the molecules strive to tilt towards the molecular director (n) which may vary for the individual molecules [2]. Liquid crystal displays (LCDs), light shutter/light modulators, light propagation waveguides, 3-D imaging active glasses, smart windows, and biosensors, etc. are some of the latest utilizations of NLCs [1,3–10]. Just like many other materials, LCs are also not ideal insulators; they have free ions in them resulting in the observance of ionic conductivity and these ions are uniformly distributed throughout the material in the absence of any external field. When an external voltage is applied there occurs a separation of charges and the formation of double layers at the surface electrodes of the LC cell. These free ions cause a dielectric loss in the material which is generally observed in the low frequency regime and are known as ionic relaxation [11]. Ions in LC material can be originated due to various reasons for instance during chemical synthesis of the LC, decomposition of the LC with time, doping of nanoparticles (which have inherent ions in them), contaminated alignment layer due to rubbing or any other process, glue used as a sealant to the LC cell, and also due to the exposure of LC material to UV, gamma, or high electric field radiations. Loads of free ions in LCs are generally undesirable because they affect the image quality (image flickering, image sticking), and induce slow response of the display devices with a reduced voltage holding ratio [12,13]. However, if we are able to control the number of ions present in LC, then it can be implemented in many practical applications like wave front corrector, low frequency oscillation generators, and so-on [11].

Tuning the ionic contamination present in the LC has been rigorous study quite lately where identification of the ionic regime exhibited by the LC material and hence its classification improves the quality of the display devices debuting in the market. There are many traditional methods that can be employed to purify the LCs from their ionic contamination for instance working with extremely purified reagents during chemical synthesis stage, vacuum sublimation, multiple recrystallizations, chromatography, electrodialysis, extraction and zone refining [14–17]. A new method that is exhibiting promising results for controlling the ionic concentration in LCs is the doping of nanoparticles. It is found that the doping of nanoparticles in LC could either decrease the concentration of ions (i.e., ion trapping regime) or increase the concentration of ions (i.e., ion releasing regime) depending on the interaction they demonstrate with the host material [18–23]. Ion trapping regime is advantageous as they tend to be useful in the long-term stability of the devices. Amongst nanoparticles, quantum dots (QDs∼ 2–10 nm) are also doped in LC to understand their effect on the ionic impurities present in the host material. Among all QDs, CdS or CdSe QDs have been much investigated and reported till now, wherein they show an ion capturing, ion releasing and a combination of ion capturing (at low temperature) and releasing (at high temperature) regimes during the investigation [11,24,25]. For instance, Rastogi et al. [24] reported that doping of core/shell Cd1-xZnxS/ZnS QDs (core diameter 5 nm and shell diameter 3.5 nm) in NLC (LC-2020) reduced the magnitude of tan δ for the mixtures which they attributed to the ion capturing phenomenon displayed by the used core-shell QDs. They mentioned that after reaching a critical concentration (0.2 wt%), QDs ion trapping behaviour was reduced. In a similar study, Rani et al. [25] demonstrated an ion trapping behaviour (decreased ionic concentration) for 0.1 and 0.17 wt% concentration of core-shell CdSe/ZnS QDs (diameter ∼5.6 nm) doped in NLC (5CB), however for concentration ≥ 0.25 wt% an ion releasing phenomenon is attributed to the increased ionic peak. In the frequency dependent conductivity curve, they came across an unusual behaviour whereby three different regions were exhibited by the composites. To gain more understanding about ion's mobility authors plotted the frequency dependent conductivity curve and explained the three different regions. The first region corresponding to the low frequency part wherein the conductivity increases with increase in frequency is attributed to the accumulation of ions at the electrode-sample interface causing electrode polarization effect. The mid frequency part is the most commonly known DC conductivity region wherein the conductivity remains invariant of the frequency. And lastly, the high frequency part wherein, a sharp increase in the conductivity occurs with frequency is accounted to the flow of ac conductivity in the composite. Konshina et al. [26] reported that addition of CdSe/ZnS QDs (diameter ∼3.5 nm) produced traps for the ions in the NLC (ZhK-1282), however under the effect of external electric field, these trapped ions left the traps causing an increase in the dielectric constant. In another study, Konshina et al. [11] reported the significant increase in the loss factor with increasing concentration of CdSe/ZnS QDs (diameter ∼5 nm, covered with trioctylphosphine oxide molecules) on the host NLC (ZhK-1289) material and attributed to the contamination induced by the ionic impurities. They also noticed an increased ionic density, however a decreased ionic mobility with concentration. It is quite noticeable that doping of CdSe/ZnS QDs are able to produce some noteworthy impact on the NLC molecules. However, we know that due to toxicity issues of Cd based QDs, the eco-friendly QDs are being preferred for application based scientific works and one of them is carbon dots (CDs) [27,28]. CDs have got a wide range of applications and is immensely used by the scientific world due to its fine properties for instance, biocompatibility, tuneable fluorescence, exceptional photostability, minimal toxicity, dielectric, etc. [29–34]. To explore the impact of CDs in the ionic relaxation of NLC, Rastogi et al. [35] dispersed oil palm leaf based carbonaceous quantum dots (OPL QDs) in the host NLC (E 48) material and reported a decrement in the tan δ peak with increasing concentration of OPL QDs. However, to the best of our knowledge, a detailed temperature, frequency, and concentration dependent dielectric study to explore the impact of CDs on the ionic relaxation and conductivity of the NLC (5CB) has not been reported. In this paper, we present a detailed frequency-temperature-concentration dependent dielectric study on the CDs-5CB composites to explore the changes observed in ionic relaxation and conductivity of 5CB due to the dopant CDs. An unprecedented shift in the ionic relaxation towards higher frequency side and also an increase in the conductivity of CDs-5CB composites are observed and attributed to the easy flow of ions as a result of induced vertical alignment in the 5CB material by the dopant CDs [36].

The host NLC, 4-cyano-4΄-pentyl biphenyl (5CB; phase sequence Cr 25N 35 Iso, Cr, N and Iso represent Crystal, Nematic and Isotropic, respectively) material (Fig. 1 (b)) procured from Sigma Aldrich was used therein without any additional purification. Organic carbon dots (CDs) of size 78 nm used as dopant in our experimental work were synthesized using one pot selective synthesis method [37] and fully characterized by Kumar group [38]. The structure of these CDs (Fig. 1(a)) and the mechanism of their formation have been recently reported by Minervini et al. [39]. CDs used in our experimental work is hydrophobic in nature because CDs, which are hydrophilic becomes unsuited for those application devices where they have to show an affinity with the weakly polar LC molecules [40]. The planar anchored ITO sample cells of 5 µm thickness were purchased from Instec, Inc. USA and used to perform dielectric measurements on pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites. We initially prepared CDs solution by dissolving them in chloroform (0.1 w/v%) whereafter, we took 5 different glass vials, and each was weighed with 5 mg of 5CB material, and a predefined volume of CDs solution was added onto these vials to prepare composites with CDs’ concentrations of 0.03wt %, 0.05 wt%, 0.1 wt%, 0.25 wt% and 0.5 wt%, respectively. These CDs-5CB composites were then sonicated for 2 hours to get a uniform dispersion of CDs in the 5CB system. Afterwards, the composites were kept inside the oven for 1.5 hours at a temperature of 65 °C for the complete evaporation of the solvent chloroform. The pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites were then loaded into empty planar anchored ITO sample cells at isotropic phase through the capillary action. It is worth pointing out here that DSC measurement confirmed the maximum reduction in clearing temperature (TN-I) of 5CB in CDs-5CB composites ∼1.1 °C and ∼1.5 °C in heating and cooling cycles, respectively [36].

Impedance analyzer is the primary instrument being used to measure the presence of ions in the LC material. So, in order to evaluate the effect of CDs on dielectric loss/dissipation factor and conductivity of the 5CB, the dielectric measurements were done using the impedance analyzer, Wayne Kerr 6500B (20 Hz-10 MHz). The temperature of the planar anchored ITO sample cells filled with pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites were controlled using temperature controller (T96, Linkam, UK) coupled with a heating/freezing stage (THMS600E, Linkam, UK) which was calibrated to an accuracy of ± 0.01 °C. To correlate the dielectric data, optical textures were checked using optical polarizing microscope (Nikon Eclipse LV100POL, Japan) connected with a CCD Q imaging camera (model: Q28378) interlinked with Linksys software.

Optical polarising microscope is a common technique used in the testing and confirmation of the alignment in LC materials. Alignment of LC remains as a salient feature for the working of display devices. Herein, Fig. 2 demonstrates the cross-polarised optical textures of planar anchored pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites, wherein increasing the concentration of CDs from 0.03 wt% to 0.5 wt% exhibits a dramatic variation in the texture (Fig. 2(b)−(f)). For instance, the 0.5 wt% CDs-5CB composite shows a uniform dark texture eventually confirming the induction of vertical alignment of planar anchored 5CB produced by CDs (Fig. 2(f)). The detailed study on such thermally stable induced vertical alignment of 5CB by CDs and the mechanism involved has already been published recently by our group [36].

Beside the textural studies, the frequency dependent dielectric studies are also performed to get a wider perspective on the molecular dynamics of pristine LCs and their composites with nanoparticles [25,41–44]. The frequency dependent dielectric permittivity at T = 28 °C shown in Fig. 3 (a) demonstrates the increased permittivity of the 5CB with concentration of CDs and which corroborates well with the observed optical textures (Fig. 2(b)−(f)). The 0.03 and 0.05 wt% composites did not show much enhancement in permittivity, however, the composites with ≥0.1 wt% shows higher values of permittivity. The highest value of dielectric permittivity obtained in the 0.5 wt% composite eventually confirms the induced vertical alignment of planar anchored 5CB by CDs. Inset of Fig. 3(a) shows the frequency-dependent dielectric loss (ε″) in which ionic relaxation is visible only for composites at T = 28 °C. The short axis relaxation at higher frequency side is observed for 0.5 wt% composite only confirming the induction of vertical alignment by CDs. It has been reported that in case of highly polar LC materials, the dielectric loss curve shows a shift to sub-hertz frequency range which eventually limits the observation of dielectric loss peak in the absorption spectrum [41]. Therefore, one would prefer to use dielectric loss factor (tan δ) spectrum to analyze the data and consequently we have used tan δ spectrum to investigate the impact of CDs on the ionic relaxation and conductivity of 5CB. Fig. 3(b) shows the frequency dependent tan δ for the pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites at T = 28 °C. As seen in the Fig. 3(b), for pristine 5CB (NLC), tan δ increases initially with increase in frequency (i.e., lower frequency region) but later on subdues substantially with further increase in frequency (i.e., higher frequency region) and is attributed to the Maxwell Wagner interfacial polarization effect [35]. The relaxation frequency (i.e., the frequency corresponding to the peak value of tan δ) for pristine 5CB is found to be more or less same as reported earlier [25].

The relation between the relaxation time ( τ ) and frequency ( f ), is given as
(1)
τ =
1
2 π f

The Eq. (1) shows that with increase in frequency, the relaxation time would be faster. The observed spectral shift in the relaxation peak towards higher frequency side with increasing concentration of CDs in 5CB is indicative of the faster relaxation of the ions present in NLC (5CB). For the 0.03 wt.% and 0.05 wt.% CDs-5CB composites, the shift in relaxation frequency could be due to the plausible decrease in the rotational viscosity of 5CB with the addition of CDs. Our observation is in line with the concept that the materials with lesser viscosity allows faster mobility of ions thereby enabling a faster relaxation of the molecules [45]. From the abatement in the amplitude of tan δ, it appears that the number of ions in the NLC (5CB) material is not increased with increase in the concentration of CDs (i.e., 0.03 and 0.05 wt%). However, for the 0.1 and 0.25 wt% CDs-5CB composites, we noticed a different trend altogether, this is due to the fact these are the intermediatory concentrations, wherein the molecular alignment of 5CB is greatly altered by the CDs [36] and which is eventually observable by the significant increment in the magnitude of tan δ. Such increment in tan δ is clearly indicative of more lossy nature of CDs-5CB composites at these concentrations and could be attributed to the strong interaction between the anchoring force of the rubbed substrates of ITO sample cell and the dopant CDs. Moreover, a tremendous redshift (∼1012 Hz) in the relaxation frequency of 0.5 wt% CDs-5CB composite (i.e., highest concentration in present study) is observed as compared to the pristine 5CB (∼86 Hz). To the best of our knowledge, such a huge shift in the relaxation frequency has not been yet reported in the literature, as usually the relaxation frequency shift produced by the dopants generally ranged between 100 and 200 Hz [46]. Therefore, such a huge shift in relaxation frequency could not only be associated to the reduction in rotational viscosity of 5CB but also to the induced vertical alignment due to the dopant CDs in the planar anchored 5CB sample cell without application of external bias field [36]. It has been observed in the past that dispersion of various nanoparticles in LC materials can produce a change in their alignment [47]. To confirm the induced vertical alignment produced by CDs, relative dielectric permittivity for pristine 5CB and for 0.5 wt% composite at 0 V and 30 V is measured (Fig. S1), and it is seen that there is no variation in the value of permittivity in the doped composite (0.5 wt%) before 0 V and after 30 V application of external electric field. The more discussion about the induction of vertical alignment of NLC (5CB) filled in a planar anchored ITO sample cells by the dopant CDs without application of external field is reported in our previous report [36].

It is reported that in the nanoparticles (dopants) doped LC composites, the ions of the LC material tend to cancel out the ions of the dopants resulting into either the quenching of the ionic peak or diminishing of the amplitude of the ionic peak [21,48,49]. Here, in case of lower concentration CDs-5CB composites (i.e., 0.03 and 0.05 wt%), the significant decrease in tan δ (i.e., ionic peak) could be due to the cancellation of ions of the 5CB by the CDs, yet due to less concentration of CDs, the total ions present in the 5CB material could not get cancelled completely. The reason for the significant rise in the ionic peak for 0.1 and 0.25 wt.% CDs-5CB composites has already been discussed. However, the highest decrement in the amplitude of the ionic peak is observed for 0.5 wt.% CDs-5CB composites and could be attributed to the increase in CDs’ concentration. In this condition, the ions present in the 5CB are cancelled out however the ions releasing from the CDs eventually resulted into the observance of the diminished and broader ionic peak. Usually, a DC voltage is applied to change the alignment of the NLC molecules from planar to vertical, in which the ions present in the NLC sweeps out towards the ITO electrodes of the sample cell eventually causing a complete suppression of the ionic relaxation observed in the frequency dependence dielectric loss (or tan δ) curve. However, in the present study, the change in molecular alignment is not done by any external applied field but it is happening due to the doping of CDs on NLC [36]. To get a better understanding about the variation caused by CDs, we analyzed Cole-Cole plot (Fig. 4 ) and found that with increasing dopant concentration, there occurred a formation of a complete semicircle which may be attributed to the broadening of the ionic peak as observed from the Fig. 3. Moreover, the inset of Fig. 4 clearly shows the complete semicircle in the high frequency region related to the short-axis molecular relaxation and which again confirms the complete vertical alignment of 5CB molecules in 0.5 wt% CDs-5CB composite.

To further explore the effect of temperature on the behavior of ionic relaxation of pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites, the temperature-dependent dielectric studies are performed and analyzed. It is observed that with increase in temperature, there is a shift in ionic relaxation towards higher frequency in all samples as shown in the Fig. 5 . A point to be noted here that the observed shift in ionic relaxation towards higher frequency with increase in temperature is due to the significant decrease in the rotational viscosity of NLC material as the sample approaches from LC phase to isotropic liquid phase. However, in CDs-5CB composites, the spectral peak of ionic relaxation also changes due to the variation of the concentration of CDs along with temperature.

Furthermore, to compare the shift in ionic relaxation frequency throughout nematic phase and few temperatures in isotropic phase of pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites, the temperature dependent ionic relaxation frequency is displayed in the Fig. 6 . The enhancement in the ionic relaxation frequency is found to be ∼13 to 18-folds in the composites as compared to pristine 5CB sample. It is clear from the graph (experimental data) that the highest increment in the relaxation frequency is observed in case of 0.5 wt% CDs-5CB composites as compared other composites and pristine 5CB sample. The trend of best linear fit also corroborates well with experimental data. Such striking result is attributed to the change of molecular alignment of 5CB sample from planar to vertical by the dopant CDs and eventually leading to the faster relaxation. Moreover, the temperature dependent ionic relaxation frequency also helps us in understanding the tuning of ionic relaxation via modulation of temperature and concentration of CDs, useful in various applications [11], wherein tuning of the ions plays a major role in operation of devices.

To understand more about the ionic relaxation and its temperature and concentration dependence in CDs-5CB composites as compared to pristine 5CB sample, the frequency-temperature-concentration dependent conductivity studies would be helpful. We are quite familiar with the basic relation between dielectric loss (ε″) and conductivity (σ)
(2)
σ = 2 π f
ε
0
ε
″

Eq. (2) clearly signifies that magnitude of conductivity can also be used to understand and explain the changes observed in ionic concentration of LC. Fig. 7 demonstrates the frequency dependent conductivity for pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites at T = 28 °C. In NLC material, the conductivity curve generally shows two regions i.e., (i) DC conductivity region: a plateau in the low frequency range and (ii) AC conductivity: an increase in the conductivity in the higher frequency side [50]. The similar trend is observed in case of pristine 5CB material; however, three different regions are observed in case of CDs-5CB composites as shown in the Fig. 7. The regions observed in the composites could be analyzed by dividing it into the three separate zones of frequency dependent electrical conductivity curve, whereby the initial increase in the magnitude of conductivity at low frequency region (20–100 Hz) can be accredited to the formation of Maxwell-Wagner-Sillar (MWS) polarization at the interface between CDs and NLC material [51]. Kumar et al. [51] has reported a similar outcome in octadecylamine functionalized CdSe/ZnS core-shell QDs (diameter ∼6 nm) doped hockey stick NLC composites and attributed to the accumulation of more and more charge carriers with decrease in frequency. In the intermediate frequency band (102–104 Hz), electrical conductivity's ionic component predominates and is generally referred to as DC conductivity region [52]. As per Jonscher's Power Law [35] total conductivity (σ) is represented as
(3)
σ =
σ i
+ A
ω
n
Here, σi corresponds to ionic conductivity, A is pre-exponential factor, ω is the angular frequency and ‘n’ denotes the fractional exponent ranging between 0 and 1. After fitting Fig. 7 with Jonscher's power law it was observed that the exponent ‘n’ value decreases with increasing concentration, surprisingly remaining beyond the Jonscher's limit (i.e. n > 1) in all samples under investigation with pristine 5CB showing the highest value of all. Similar outcome was reported by Rani et al. [25] also in CdSe/ZnS QDs doped 5CB composites. At low frequency region, the dipolar part (Aωn) of Eq. (3) can be overlooked, which makes the total conductivity to be equivalent to ionic conductivity (
σ i
) [53]. Therefore, the value of
σ i
from the fitted graph is found to be 2.537 × 10−7 (S/m) and 4.6127 × 10−6(S/m) for pristine 5CB and 0.5 wt% CDs-5CB composite (i.e., highest concentration), respectively (Fig. 7). This clearly shows ∼18 folds enhancement in
σ i
of 5CB sample after doping of 0.5 wt% CDs. However, for the composites with lower concentrations of CDs (0.03, 0.05, and 0.1 wt%), the fairly increased conductivity is observed and attributed to the reduced viscosity of 5CB material due to the dopant CDs [44,54]. This corroborates well with the fact that the conductivity is inversely proportional to the viscosity (
σ i
α 1/viscosity). Obviously, the ions tend to move more freely in a material having less viscosity than the ones with higher viscosity. From Fig. 3, one can see the decrease in tan δ value and which clearly indicates that ionic concentration is decreased due to the dopant CDs. Therefore, it appears that increasing conductivity (or current flow) is not related to the enhanced ionic concentration but could be attributed to the increased flow of the ions present in the composite as a result of lower viscosity. However, the anomaly observed in the 0.25 wt% composite (Figs. 3 and 7) could be due to the induced instability in the planar alignment of 5CB in a planar anchored sample cell due to the dopant CDs. In other words, one could say that sample cell is in the state, wherein the competition of overcoming the planar anchoring of 5CB by dopant CDs is at peak and hence it would be a chaotic situation and eventually increase the lossy nature of 5CB (i.e., tanδ value). Interestingly, the further increase in concentration of CDs (i.e., >0.25 wt%) the existing planar alignment of LC cell is fully converted into the vertical alignment [36]. Hence, we see a significant enhancement in the value of conductivity for 0.5 wt% CDs-5CB composite as compared to pristine 5CB as the vertical alignment produces an easier path for the flow of ions in 5CB matrix. Kondratenko et al. [55] has discussed about the effect of planar and vertical alignment on the ionic conductivity of the liquid crystal material (5CB). They pointed out that the displacement of ions in the planar anchored sample cells is perpendicular to the NLC director (n) and that eventually poses as an additional hassle in the ionic transportation due to appreciable amount of rotational viscosity in the 5CB. However, in the case of vertical alignment, the NLC director (n) is parallel to the applied electric field E which aids the transportation of the ions. Thus, the same effect is observed in our conductivity results for the pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites. We have demonstrated the above mechanism through a schematic diagram in Fig. 8 .

Variation of the ionic conductivity with inverse of absolute temperature has been plotted in Fig. 9 and fitting was performed to satisfy the Arrhenius equation [35]:
(4)
σ i
=
σ o
e x p
(
−
E a
/
k b
T
)
(5)
l n
(
σ i
)
= l n
(
σ o
)
−
E a
/
k b
T
here, σo is the pre-exponential factor, Ea denotes the activation energy, kb being the Boltzmann constant and T represents the temperature. The addition of CDs demonstrates a linear increment in the value of conductivity with increase in temperature through Arrhenius plot. For instance, at 28 °C (deep nematic), the conductivity is found to be 0.25 × 10−6S/m and 4.61 × 10−6S/m for pristine 5CB and 0.5 wt% CDs-5CB composites, respectively. Similarly at 34 °C (close to transition), the conductivity value is observed to be 0.36 × 10−6S/m and 7.05 × 10−6S/m for pristine 5CB and 0.5 wt% CDs-5CB composites, respectively. 0.5 wt% composite shows the highest marginal increase in the conductivity facilitating the mobility of ions due to the induced vertical alignment along with the inclusion of external ions by CDs. Nevertheless, it is also worth pointing out here that the variation of conductivity with respect to dopant concentration is same except for 0.25 wt% CDs-5CB composite (i.e., conductivity of 5CB increases with increase in concentration of CDs). This could be explained by considering the fact that conductivity also linearly depends on the mobility of ions. The magnitude of mobility depends on how easily ions (i.e., charge carriers) could move inside that material (here 5CB and CDs-5CB composites). To be more specific, in the present case, it would eventually depend on the alignment state of 5CB molecules. Since, alignment is neither planar nor vertical (Fig. 2e) i.e., distorted alignment state (intermediate state) in case of 0.25 wt% CDs-5CB composite, and which hinders the mobility of ions (i.e., reduced mobility) and eventually results into the observed decrement in the conductivity as compared to the 0.1 wt% CDs-5CB composite yet remains higher than pristine 5CB (Figs. 7 and 9).

The concentration dependent activation energy (Ea) derived from the Arrhenius plot is shown in Fig. 10
. The value of Ea for pristine 5CB (0.66 eV) is observed to be the highest and then it shows a decreasing trend for lower concentrations (0.03 &0.05 wt%). Further increment in concentrations (≥0.1 wt%) the value of Ea increases slightly yet remains lesser than the value for pristine 5CB [56]. The lowering value of Ea can be attributed to the cancellation of ions of pristine 5CB by the addition of CDs, and consecutively the increment in Ea can be accredited to the inclusion of surplus external ions by CDs at higher concentrations.

Firstly, the effect of dopant CDs on the planar aligned 5CB, NLC is investigated through the optical polarising microscope and confirmed the complete conversion of planar to vertical alignment of 5CB in 0.5 wt% CDs-5CB composite. Afterwards, the detailed frequency, temperature, and concentration dependent dielectric studies on pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites using the high-resolution dielectric spectroscopy are carried out in order to understand the impact of dopant CDs on the ionic behaviour of 5CB throughout its nematic and isotropic phases. The significant shift in the ionic relaxation frequency towards higher frequency in CDs-5CB composites with increase in temperature as well as concentration of the dopant CDs is observed. For instance, the shift in ionic relaxation frequency for 0.5 wt% CDs-5CB composite as compared to pristine 5CB sample is found to be ∼13 folds at 28 °C (nematic) and further become ∼18 folds at 38 °C (isotropic). The similar trend is also observed in frequency-dependent conductivity data. The significant shift in ionic relaxation frequency and enhancement in the conductivity of 0.5 wt% CDs-5CB composite are attributed to the induced vertical alignment in host 5CB material by the dopant CDs. The temperature dependent studies also confirm (i) the shift in ionic relaxation frequency and (ii) enhancement in the conductivity of 5CB and its composite shown through Arrhenius plot. This could be attributed to the decrease in the rotational viscosity of sample with increase in temperature. Moreover, the activation energy related to ionic conductivity evaluated from Arrhenius plot is found to be highly dependent on the dopant concentration. The present study would certainly help us in understanding the tuning of ions present in the LC sample cells filled with pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites. It's worth pointing out here that the tuning of ionic concentration in 5CB by the dopant CDs would have many applications such as liquid crystal wave front corrector, optical switches based on dynamic light scattering, electrically controlled ionic modification of surface anchoring, and liquid crystal generator of low frequency oscillation [11]. Moreover, the further detailed study should be done on pristine 5CB and CDs-5CB composites using various characterization techniques to evaluate and compare the values of ion density and ion mobility for a better understanding on the behaviour of ions.

Priscilla P: Data curation, Investigation, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Ashwani Kumar Singh: Data curation, Formal analysis. Praveen Malik: Writing – review & editing. Sandeep Kumar: Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Supreet: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Arvind K. Gathania: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Jai Prakash: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Riccardo Castagna: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Daniele Eugenio Lucchetta: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Poonma Malik: Writing – review & editing. Gautam Singh: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the authors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors Priscilla P and Gautam Singh thank to Amity University Uttar Pradesh, Noida, India for its continuous support and encouragement in their research work. Priscilla P would like to thank UGC for the fellowship awarded under the scheme Savitribai Jyotirao Phule Single Girl Child Fellowship (SJSGC 2022-2023). Jai Prakash and Gautam Singh are grateful to the Council of Science and Technology (CST), U. P., India for financial assistance under CST funded research project (Project ID: 1960). Supreet would like to acknowledge the support provided under the DST-FIST Grant No.SR/FST/PS-I/2019/68 of Govt. of India.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2023.137403.

J. Mol. Liq.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Kumar, V. Prasad, M. Manjunath, Quantum dots dispersed hockey stick nematic liquid crystal: studies on dielectric permittivity, elastic constants and electrical conductivity, J. Mol. Liq. 266,10–18 (2018).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dielectric and electrical properties of nematic liquid crystals 6CB doped with iron oxide nanoparticles. The combined effect of nanodopant concentration and cell thickness
J. Mol. Liq.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O.V. Kovalchuk, T.M. Kovalchuk, N. Tomašovičová, M. Timko, K. Zakutanska, D. Miakota, P. Kopčanský, O.F. Shevchuk, and Y. Garbovskiy, Dielectric and electrical properties of nematic liquid crystals 6CB doped with iron oxide nanoparticles. The combined effect of nanodopant concentration and cell thickness, J. Mol. Liq. 366, 120305 (2022).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Boost in the thermal stability, ionic conductivity and director relaxation frequency in the composite of liquid crystal and functionalised multi-walled carbon nanotubes
Liq. Cryst.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.K. Patro, R. Verma, A. Garg, R. Dhar, and R. Dabrowski, Boost in the thermal stability, ionic conductivity and director relaxation frequency in the composite of liquid crystal and functionalised multi-walled carbon nanotubes, Liq. Cryst. 48, 345 (2020).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A field-reversal method for measuring the parameters of a ferroelectric liquid crystal
Liq. Cryst.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Panov, J.K. Vij and N.M. Shtykov, A field-reversal method for measuring the parameters of a ferroelectric liquid crystal, Liq. Cryst.. 28, 615–620 (2001).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ionic transport in nematic liquid crystals and alignment layer effects on electrode polarization
J. Chem. Phys.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Kondratenko, Y. Boussoualem, S. Longbeard, and A. Daoudi, Ionic transport in nematic liquid crystals and alignment layer effects on electrode polarization, J. Chem. Phys. 149, 134902 (2018).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zinc oleate nanorod-induced vertical alignment of nematic liquid crystal
ACS Omega

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Mishra, V. Manjuladevi, and R.K. Gupta, Zinc oleate nanorod-induced vertical alignment of nematic liquid crystal, ACS Omega. 7, 46466–46474 (2022).
